content: The streets in the Indus Valley civilization were well-planned, with a gridiron layout of main streets running north-south and east-west, dividing the area into blocks of roughly equal size. The main streets were about 30 feet wide, and the lanes were narrower, typically between 5 to 10 feet wide. The streets had a well-planned drainage system, with pipes and carefully built chutes to prevent overflow or splashing. Some streets had small single rooms, likely used as guard posts or night-watchmen's quarters, and were accessible from the streets. The streets also had access to numerous wells, some of which were incorporated into the houses.

Trade between the Indus Valley civilization and Sumeria is referenced in various texts, with evidence of trade found in the Persian Gulf, including seals dated to 1923 B.C. This trade seems to have culminated under Sargon of Agade around 2350 B.C., dwindled during the end of the Agade dynasty, and then revived under King Ur-Nammu. However, the surviving evidence of this interchange is not impressive in bulk, and it is likely that many inter-regional resemblances, particularly in matters of religion, owe more to community of inheritance than to trade.
